1:
"Now when he had ended all his sayings in the audience of the people, he entered into Capernaum." The witnesses note the transition from the Sermon on the Plain to the miracles, and a question of harmony. Titus of Bostra reads the order: "when He had strengthened His disciples by more perfect teaching, He goes to Capernaum to work miracles there." Augustine guards the chronology: "He did not enter before He had ended these sayings, but it is not mentioned what space of time intervened... for in that interval the leper was cleansed whom Matthew introduced in his proper place." Ambrose draws the fitness: "having finished His teaching, He rightly instructs them to follow the example of His precepts; for straightway the servant of a Gentile centurion is presented to the Lord to be healed."

Eusebius notes the centurion's discernment: though "strong in battle, and prefect of the Roman soldiers," he "judged that these miracles were performed by no human power, and sends to Him as to God, not looking to the visible instrument." Augustine and Robertson take up the apparent discrepancy with Matthew (who says the centurion "came"), to be addressed in the verses following.

So the witnesses make the verse the bridge from word to deed. The lesson the expositors draw is the fitting union of Christ's teaching and his works. Having "ended all his sayings" — the great discourse of precepts — Jesus enters Capernaum and at once confirms his words by deeds, healing the centurion's servant. The fathers find a deliberate pattern: the Teacher, having laid down the rule of life, immediately illustrates it, and his first act is mercy to a Gentile, foreshadowing the gospel's reach beyond Israel. Augustine's careful note, the witnesses observe, preserves the harmony of the Gospels: Luke does not date the entry to the very moment the sermon ended, leaving room for the events Matthew records in between, so that the evangelists, rightly read, do not contradict but complement one another. Eusebius's observation, the expositors press, already marks the centurion's faith: a Roman officer, accustomed to power, perceives in Christ a power more than human and approaches him as one would approach God. The verse thus closes the sermon and opens the section of mighty works, in which Christ's authority over disease, death, and sin will be displayed — beginning with the faith of a Gentile soldier whose servant lay sick unto death.
2:
"And a certain centurion's servant, who was dear unto him, was sick, and ready to die." The witnesses note the servant's value and the master's regard. The grammatical-historical Ellicott weighs the phrase: "was precious, the dearness of value, but not necessarily of affection," and observes that Luke, the physician, "is here less precise than St. Matthew, who states that the slave was sick of the palsy" — perhaps because Luke "had made inquiries" and supplies other details Matthew lacks. Barnes glosses "dear" as "valuable, trusty, and honoured." Lightfoot supplies a rabbinic parallel: the upright servant Tabi, for whom his master Rabban Gamaliel received consolations, "was not as other servants, he was most upright."

The witnesses note the contrast with the common estimate of slaves: this servant was prized by his master, whether for his fidelity, his worth, or genuine affection — a regard that moved the centurion to seek his healing as earnestly as for a son.

So the witnesses make the verse the setting of the miracle and a testimony to the centurion's character. The lesson the expositors draw is the humanity and worth that bound master and servant. The servant was "dear" — held precious, whether for his usefulness, his uprightness (as the rabbinic Tabi), or true love; and the master's care for a slave at the point of death, the witnesses note, already marks the centurion as a man of unusual tenderness, far above the callous estimate of slaves common in the ancient world. Ellicott's observation about Luke's medical interest, the expositors press, is characteristic: the physician-evangelist, though here vaguer than Matthew on the disease, elsewhere supplies details that show he had inquired into the case. The servant being "ready to die," the witnesses note, sets the stage for the urgency of the embassy and the greatness of the cure: this was no light ailment but a mortal sickness, and the centurion's faith would be tested by the seeming hopelessness of the case. The verse thus introduces the suffering servant and the caring master, and prepares for the remarkable faith and humility the Gentile soldier was about to display, in seeking from Christ the life of one whom he so valued.
3:
"And when he heard of Jesus, he sent unto him the elders of the Jews, beseeching him that he would come and heal his servant." The witnesses note the embassy of elders and the harmony with Matthew. The grammatical-historical Clarke explains: the elders were "either magistrates, or the elders of the synagogue which the centurion had built; he sent these, probably, because he was afraid to come to Christ himself, not being a Jew." On the discrepancy with Matthew (who says the centurion "came"), Clarke gives the principle: "it is a usual form of speech in all nations to attribute the act to a person which is done by his authority." Robertson concurs: "what one does through another he does himself," and suggests "Luke tells the story as it happened with the details, whereas Matthew simply presents a summary statement." Ellicott notes "elders" has no article — "not the whole body" — and that this detail, "peculiar to St. Luke," shows the centurion was "like Cornelius, at least half a proselyte."

The witnesses note the humility already at work: the centurion does not approach Christ directly but sends the Jewish elders as intercessors, esteeming himself unworthy.

So the witnesses make the verse the first of the centurion's embassies and a classic case of Gospel harmony. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the reconciliation of Matthew and Luke: the centurion is said by Matthew to "come" because he acted through the elders and friends he sent — a man does through his agents what he is said to do himself — while Luke, with his fuller detail, records the actual embassies. There is no contradiction, the witnesses press, but the natural difference between a summary and a detailed account. Second, the centurion's character: that he sent the elders of the Jews, who pleaded his cause, shows both his connection with the synagogue (he had built it, half a proselyte like Cornelius) and his humility, for he dared not approach Christ in person. The witnesses note the irony that a Gentile soldier should so reverence the Jewish prophet that he employs the Jewish elders as his mediators. The verse thus opens the account of the centurion's faith, displayed first in this humble, indirect approach, and prepares for the still deeper humility of the message he would shortly send.
4:
"And when they came to Jesus, they besought him instantly, saying, That he was worthy for whom he should do this." The witnesses note the earnestness of the elders' plea and the ground they urged. The grammatical-historical Robertson draws out the imperfect tense: "began and kept on beseeching," and the adverb "instantly" (better "earnestly," from a word meaning haste) — "so eagerly, zealously, for time was short." Barnes notes the plea: "they besought him urgently; he was worthy, the centurion; he had showed favour to the Jews, and it was not improper to show him a kindness." Ellicott observes "instantly" has lost its older meaning of "urgently."

The elders, the witnesses note, pleaded the centurion's worthiness — a striking contrast to the centurion's own estimate of himself, which the verses to come would reveal. They thought him deserving; he thought himself unworthy.

So the witnesses make the verse a study in the elders' earnest intercession and the difference between their estimate and the centurion's. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between the worthiness urged by others and the unworthiness felt by the man himself. The Jewish elders pressed Christ urgently — beginning and continuing their plea, eager because the servant was dying — and they grounded their request on the centurion's merit: "he was worthy," for he loved the nation and had built the synagogue. The witnesses note the irony the narrative will soon unfold: the very man whom the elders called "worthy" would shortly send word that he was not worthy even to receive Christ under his roof, nor to come to him in person. Their plea rested on his good works; his own faith rested on his sense of unworthiness and Christ's power. The expositors press the lesson: those most truly worthy in the eyes of others are often those who feel themselves least worthy before God; and the centurion's greatness of faith, which Christ would marvel at, was bound up with this very humility. The verse thus shows the elders pleading the man's deserts, even as the man himself was about to disclaim all deserving — the warm intercession of friends meeting the deeper humility of faith.
5:
"For he loveth our nation, and he hath built us a synagogue." The witnesses note the grounds of the elders' commendation and the centurion's piety. The Reformed Gill marks the rarity of such love: "it was not common for the Gentiles to love the Jews... there was an hatred, yea, an enmity between them; but this man, very likely, was a proselyte to their religion." Lightfoot supplies the background: "it was no unusual thing for one single man to build a synagogue at his own charge," and the Jews "had no scruple as to a Gentile's building it, since the holiness consisted not so much in the building as in its being set apart to holy use."

The elders, the witnesses note, urged two proofs of the centurion's worth: he "loveth our nation" — a rare affection in a Gentile, especially a Roman officer — and "he hath built us a synagogue," a costly act of devotion to the worship of Israel's God.

So the witnesses make the verse the evidence of the centurion's exceptional character. The lesson the expositors draw is the genuine piety of this Gentile, expressed in love for God's people and generosity to his worship. The centurion's love for the Jewish nation, the witnesses note, was remarkable: where Jew and Gentile commonly met in mutual contempt, this Roman soldier had come to love Israel — a token, the expositors judge, that he had embraced their religion, at least as a proselyte of the gate, drawn to the true God whom they served. And his building of the synagogue, Lightfoot shows, was a substantial act of devotion, a wealthy man's gift to the public worship of God, dedicating his means to holy use. The witnesses note that such works, while they could not earn salvation, were the fruit of a heart already turned toward God, and prepared the way for the faith he would show in Christ. The elders rightly saw in them the marks of an unusual man; yet, the expositors press, the centurion's true greatness lay not in these works — which he himself would not plead — but in the humble faith that owned Christ's authority over disease with a word. The verse thus paints the Gentile soldier as a lover of God's people and a builder of his house, whose outward piety was crowned by the inward faith that Christ would marvel at.
6:
"Then Jesus went with them. And when he was now not far from the house, the centurion sent friends to him, saying unto him, Lord, trouble not thyself: for I am not worthy that thou shouldest enter under my roof." The witnesses note Christ's ready compliance and the centurion's second, deeper embassy. The grammatical-historical Gill marks the willingness: Jesus "at once complied, made no hesitation, or difficulty about it, but went with them very freely." Robertson notes the imperfect — "he started to go along with them" — and that "this second embassy also, wanting in Matthew's narrative," shows Luke's fuller detail; Matthew "puts the message of both into the mouth of the centurion himself." Ellicott finds the second embassy "more true to nature: the centurion was not likely to leave the slave so precious to him when he seemed in the very agonies of death." On "trouble not thyself," Robertson notes the verb originally meant "to flay, to mangle," then "to vex, annoy."

The centurion's word, the witnesses note, breathes humility: "I am not worthy that thou shouldest enter under my roof" — esteeming himself unfit to receive the Holy One into his Gentile house.

So the witnesses make the verse the meeting of Christ's gracious readiness with the centurion's profound humility. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the willingness of Christ: at the elders' plea he set out at once for the house of a Gentile, making no objection — a readiness that, the witnesses note, already answered the centurion's need before the second message came. Second, the humility of the centurion: as Christ drew near, the soldier sent friends to stop him, not from indifference but from a sense of unworthiness — "I am not worthy that thou shouldest enter under my roof." The witnesses press the depth of this: the man whom the elders had called "worthy" disclaims all worthiness, shrinking from the honour of receiving Christ into his home, and unwilling to "trouble" the Lord on his account. Ellicott's note, the expositors observe, adds a touching realism: the centurion did not himself leave his dying servant, but sent friends with the message. The verse thus displays the two graces that crown the story — Christ's condescension, going freely to a Gentile's house, and the centurion's lowliness, counting himself unfit for so great a guest — the very humility that was the soil of his great faith.
7:
"Wherefore neither thought I myself worthy to come unto thee: but say in a word, and my servant shall be healed." The witnesses dwell on the climax of the centurion's humility and faith. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the depth: "the humility of the centurion appears in a yet stronger light than in St. Matthew's report; far from expecting the Prophet to come under his roof, he had not dared even to approach Him." Robertson notes this clause is "not in Matthew, because he represents the centurion as coming to Jesus," and renders "speak with a word" — the imperative "let my servant be healed." Barnes connects the faith to Scripture: "he sent his word, and healed them" (Psalm 107:20). The word for "servant" here (pais, "boy"), Robertson notes, is "an affectionate term for the slave who was dear to him."

The centurion's faith, the witnesses agree, rises to its height: he believes Christ can heal by a mere word, at a distance, without coming or touching — a confidence in the bare authority of Christ's command.

So the witnesses make the verse the summit of the centurion's faith and humility together. The lesson the expositors draw is the union of the deepest lowliness with the highest faith. The centurion, the witnesses note, sinks lower still: he had not thought himself worthy even "to come unto thee" — not only unworthy to receive Christ, but unworthy to approach him in person, which is why he sent the elders and then his friends. Yet out of this depth of humility rises a faith higher than any in Israel: "say in a word, and my servant shall be healed." He asks no visit, no touch, no presence — only the word of command, believing that Christ's bare authority can heal at a distance, as a general's order is obeyed though he be far off. The witnesses press the greatness of this: the centurion grasped, more clearly than the Jews, the absolute power of Christ's word over disease, needing no contact, bound by no distance. His humility and his faith, the expositors observe, are not opposed but joined: it is the man who feels most his own unworthiness who trusts most wholly in Christ's power. The verse thus sets forth the faith that Christ would marvel at — a Gentile's confidence in the omnipotent word of the Lord, sprung from a heart that owned its own unworthiness and rested all on Christ.
8:
"For I also am a man set under authority, having under me soldiers, and I say unto one, Go, and he goeth; and to another, Come, and he cometh; and to my servant, Do this, and he doeth it." The witnesses expound the centurion's military reasoning. The Reformed Gill notes the force of "set under authority": "even he who was but an inferior officer," under the command of a tribune, "yet had such power as after related" — so that the argument is "from the lesser to the greater." Robertson confirms the reading "set" (tassomenos) is genuine here, "this vivid and characteristic speech of the centurion."

The centurion reasons, the witnesses agree, from his own experience of command: he, a subordinate officer, has soldiers and a servant who obey his word instantly; how much more shall Christ, the supreme authority over disease, be obeyed by sickness at his mere command. The diseases are, as it were, his soldiers, going and coming at his word.

So the witnesses make the verse the ground of the centurion's remarkable faith. The lesson the expositors draw is the spiritual insight of the soldier, who from his own office grasped the authority of Christ. The centurion, the witnesses note, argues from the lesser to the greater: if he, a mere subaltern under higher command, can yet order his soldiers and servant and be obeyed without question — "Go, and he goeth; Come, and he cometh" — how much more can Christ, who holds supreme and unlimited authority, command disease and death and be instantly obeyed. Robertson's observation, the expositors press, illuminates the faith: as a military man, the centurion "had learned how to receive orders and to execute them, and hence to expect obedience to his commands; he recognized Jesus as Master over disease, with power to compel obedience." The diseases, in the centurion's view, are like Christ's soldiers, coming and going at his word, so that a single command suffices to heal, without his presence. The witnesses note the depth of this faith: it conceived of Christ's authority over the unseen world of sickness as absolute and immediate, like — yet infinitely beyond — a commander's authority over his troops. The verse thus reveals the reasoning behind the centurion's confidence, an analogy drawn from his own calling that led him to a truer grasp of Christ's power than the Israel he served among.
9:
"When Jesus heard these things, he marvelled at him, and turned him about, and said unto the people that followed him, I say unto you, I have not found so great faith, no, not in Israel." The witnesses dwell on Christ's marvelling and his praise of Gentile faith. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the vivid detail peculiar to Luke: "turned (second aorist passive participle) — a vivid touch not in Matthew's account. In both Matthew and Luke Jesus marvels at the great faith of this Roman centurion beyond that among the Jews." Ellicott observes that "St. Luke omits the warning words which St. Matthew records as to the many that shall come from the east and the west, and the exclusion of the children of the kingdom."

That Christ "marvelled," the witnesses note, is striking — the Lord, who knew all things, yet expresses wonder at the faith of this Gentile, holding it up as exceeding any he had found in Israel, the covenant people.

So the witnesses make the verse the divine commendation of the centurion's faith. The lesson the expositors draw is the greatness of a faith that surpassed Israel's, found in a Gentile. Christ "marvelled" — a wonder, the witnesses note, not of ignorance but of approval, the Lord drawing attention to a faith so remarkable that he turned to the following crowd to praise it. And his praise is pointed: "I have not found so great faith, no, not in Israel" — among the people of the covenant, who had the law, the prophets, and the promises, none had shown such confidence in his power as this Roman soldier. The witnesses press the rebuke implied: those who had every advantage believed less than the Gentile who had so few. Robertson's note on the vivid "turned," the expositors observe, marks Luke's eyewitness vividness — Christ wheeling round to commend the centurion to the crowd. Luke's omission of the warning about the children of the kingdom cast out, the witnesses note, leaves the emphasis on the praise itself, though Matthew's fuller report shows the warning was given: that Gentiles of such faith should sit down in the kingdom while unbelieving Israel was excluded. The verse thus holds up the centurion as the pattern of faith — a Gentile whose trust in Christ's word exceeded all that Christ had found among his own people — and Christ's marvelling commendation as the seal upon it.
10:
"And they that were sent, returning to the house, found the servant whole that had been sick." The witnesses note the completed cure and its instrument. The grammatical-historical Gill identifies those returning as "both the elders of the Jews and the friends of the centurion." Robertson and Ellicott note Luke's medical term for "whole" (hygiainonta, "sound, well," cf. Luke 5:31), "characteristic" of the physician-evangelist. Adam Clarke draws out the theology of the cure: "this cure was the effect of the faith, prayer, and humility of the centurion, through which the almighty energy of Jesus Christ was conveyed to the sick man; but these very graces in the centurion were the products of grace." He presses: "it is God himself who, by the gifts of his mercy, disposes the soul to receive its cure... we cannot endue ourselves with a gracious disposition, yet we can quench the Spirit" (Titus 2:11).

The servant, the witnesses note, was found "whole" — completely restored — at the very hour Christ spoke the word, confirming the centurion's faith and the power of Christ's command at a distance.

So the witnesses make the verse the seal of the miracle and the reward of faith. The lesson the expositors draw is the efficacy of Christ's word and the source of saving faith. The messengers returned to find the servant "whole" — the cure accomplished by the bare word of Christ, spoken at a distance, exactly as the centurion had believed; the faith that asked "say in a word" was answered to the letter. The witnesses note Luke's precise medical term for the recovery, the physician noting the soundness of the healed man. But Clarke's deeper reflection, the expositors press, lifts the verse to its doctrinal height: the cure was the fruit of the centurion's faith, prayer, and humility — yet these very graces were themselves the gift of God, "the products of grace." The faith that obtained the miracle was not the centurion's own achievement but God's work in him; for it is God who disposes the soul to receive his mercy, and no man can give himself a gracious disposition, though he may quench the Spirit who works it. The verse thus closes the account with the servant healed and the centurion's faith vindicated, and reminds the reader that the faith which lays hold of Christ's power is itself the grace of God — the same grace that, appearing to all men, brings salvation, and that the centurion had not received in vain.
11:
"And it came to pass the day after, that he went into a city called Nain; and many of his disciples went with him, and much people." The witnesses introduce the raising of the widow's son, recorded by Luke alone, and note its place in the order of miracles. Cyril marks the progression: "the Lord joins one miracle upon another. In the former instance He came indeed when called for, but in this He came self-invited." Gregory of Nyssa sees a deliberate ascent: "beginning His miracles with the less wonderful, He reconciled our faith to far greater. First in the grievous sickness of the centurion's servant He verged upon the power of resurrection; afterwards with a higher power He led men to belief in a resurrection, when He raised the widow's son." Titus of Bostra notes the answer to the cavil that the centurion's servant "was not going to die": Luke "explains that the young man was already dead."

Bede notes Nain was "a city of Galilee, within two miles of mount Tabor," and that "by the divine counsel there were large multitudes accompanying the Lord, that there might be many witnesses of so great a miracle."

So the witnesses make the verse the threshold of Christ's first recorded raising of the dead and a step in the ascending revelation of his power. The lesson the expositors draw is the wisdom of the order in which Christ wrought his miracles. The fathers note a deliberate progression: Christ moved from healing the sick (the centurion's servant, near death) to raising the dead (the widow's son, certainly dead, carried to burial), and would later raise Lazarus, four days in the tomb — each miracle leading faith upward to the next, until men could believe the general resurrection. The witnesses note, with Cyril, the unbidden grace of this miracle: at Capernaum Christ was summoned; at Nain he comes "self-invited," moved by his own compassion, no one having asked. And Bede's observation, the expositors press, marks the providence in the crowds: the great multitude accompanying Christ ensured many witnesses to so public and undeniable a wonder. The verse thus sets the scene — Christ approaching the gate of Nain with his disciples and a throng — for the meeting of the procession of life with the procession of death, in which the Lord of life would display, unasked, his power over the grave.
12:
"Now when he came nigh to the gate of the city, behold, there was a dead man carried out, the only son of his mother, and she was a widow: and much people of the city was with her." The witnesses dwell on the depth of the widow's misery. Gregory of Nyssa compresses it: "He has told us the sum of misery in a few words. The mother was a widow, and had no hope of bearing other sons; she could find no one to look to in place of him she had lost." Lightfoot supplies the burial customs: "a dead corpse going out" meant carrying it "out of the court-gate" and then "out of the city, for the burying-places were not near the city"; and "if the deceased be known to many, then many accompany him." Barnes notes the gate was in the city wall, through which the funeral passed.

The witnesses note the threefold sorrow: the man was dead; he was an "only son"; and his mother was a "widow" — bereft of husband and now of her sole child, her last earthly stay.

So the witnesses make the verse a portrait of the deepest human grief, set against the coming display of Christ's power. The lesson the expositors draw is the completeness of the widow's calamity, which moved the compassion of Christ. The evangelist, the witnesses note, heaps up the marks of sorrow: a dead man borne to burial; the "only son" of his mother, so that her whole hope of posterity died with him; and the mother herself a "widow," already bereaved of her husband, now losing her last comfort and support. Gregory of Nyssa's reading, the expositors press, captures the desolation — she had no hope of other sons, no one to replace the lost; her grief was total. The great crowd accompanying her, Lightfoot shows, accords with the custom that many mourners followed one known and pitied; and so the funeral train was large, matching the multitude with Christ. The witnesses note the providential meeting at the gate: the procession of death, with its weeping widow, met the procession of life, with the Lord of life at its head. The verse thus sets in the sharpest relief the misery that Christ was about to relieve — a widowed mother following her only son to the grave — and prepares for the compassion, unasked and unbidden, with which the Saviour would meet her at the city gate.
13:
"And when the Lord saw her, he had compassion on her, and said unto her, Weep not." The witnesses dwell on Christ's unbidden compassion and a notable title. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks "the Lord" used absolutely: "one of the comparatively few instances in which the term the Lord is used absolutely instead of Jesus... it confirms that the narrative came from those who had a profound reverence for the Master," a usage "more frequent in St. Luke and St. John." On the compassion, he notes "how our Lord's works of wonder spring not from a distinct purpose to offer credentials of His mission, but from the outflow of a tender human sympathy."

Gill notes Christ acted "knowing her case, that she was a widow, and had lost her only son" — his compassion springing from full knowledge of her grief. The "Weep not," the witnesses agree, was no idle comfort but the prelude to the act that would dry her tears.

So the witnesses make the verse the welling-up of Christ's compassion, the spring of the miracle. The lesson the expositors draw is the tender, spontaneous mercy of the Lord. No one asked Christ to intervene; he "saw her" — the weeping widow following her only son — and "had compassion," his heart moved by her grief before any plea was made. The witnesses note, with Ellicott, that the miracle flowed not from a calculated wish to prove his mission but from the genuine outflow of human sympathy; Christ wept with those who wept, and his power served his pity. His word "Weep not," the expositors press, was no hollow consolation, such as men offer who cannot remove the cause of grief; it was the word of One about to raise the dead, who could bid the mother dry her tears because he was about to restore her son. The title "the Lord," used here absolutely, the witnesses observe, breathes the reverence of the disciples who told the story, and points already to the divine power soon to be shown. The verse thus reveals the heart from which the miracle sprang — the compassion of the Lord, who, meeting unasked the deepest human sorrow, was moved to pity and to power, bidding the widow weep no more because he would turn her mourning into joy.
14:
"And he came and touched the bier: and they that bare him stood still. And he said, Young man, I say unto thee, Arise." The witnesses dwell on the touch, the halting bearers, and the word of power. The grammatical-historical Ellicott describes the bier: "not a closed-up coffin, but an open bier on which the corpse lay wrapped in its winding-sheet," as with Lazarus (John 11:44) and the Lord himself (John 20:6-7); "the immediate effect of the touch was that they who bore the bier stood still." Lightfoot notes the Jewish dread of contact with the dead — "beware that no uncircumcised person touch my bed, lest he drive away the Divine presence" — which Christ overrides, contracting no defilement but imparting life. Barnes cross-references the raisings by his word (Luke 8:54; Acts 9:40; Romans 4:17).

The witnesses note the majesty of the command: "Young man, I say unto thee, Arise" — Christ speaks to the dead as to the living, with the authority of the Lord of life, addressing the corpse with a word it must obey.

So the witnesses make the verse the act of the miracle and the display of Christ's power over death. The lesson the expositors draw is the sovereign authority of the Lord of life. Christ "touched the bier" — and the bearers, the witnesses note, "stood still," arrested by the strange action of one who, against all custom, laid his hand on the place of the dead. By that touch, the expositors press, Christ did not contract the defilement the law attached to corpses, but rather, as the Holy One, imparted life where death reigned — the living Lord overcoming death by contact, as elsewhere he cleansed the leper by touch without himself being defiled. And his word, "Young man, I say unto thee, Arise," the witnesses note, is spoken with divine authority, addressing the dead as though he could hear and obey — and indeed he must, for the voice of the Lord of life calls the departed back. The emphatic "I say unto thee," the expositors observe, marks the power as Christ's own, by his own word, not by prayer to another, as Elijah and Elisha had raised the dead. The verse thus reveals Christ as the Master of death itself, halting the funeral by his touch and recalling the dead by his word — a display of the same authority by which, at the last day, all that are in the graves shall hear his voice and come forth.
15:
"And he that was dead sat up, and began to speak. And he delivered him to his mother." The witnesses note the proofs of life and the tenderness of the restoration. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Luke's medical term: "sat up (the verb in the N.T. only here and Acts 9:40); medical writers often used it of the sick sitting up in bed." On "gave him to his mother," he calls it "a tender way of putting it," citing Bengel: "for he had already ceased to belong to his mother." Ellicott deepens it: "the mother received her son as given to her once again, God-given, in a higher sense than when she had rejoiced that a man-child was born into the world." Barnes cross-references the raisings (2 Kings 4:32-37; John 11:44).

The witnesses note the double proof of restored life — the young man "sat up" and "began to speak" — and the touching act by which Christ "delivered him to his mother," restoring not only the son but the mother's comfort.

So the witnesses make the verse the seal of the miracle and a picture of Christ's tender mercy. The lesson the expositors draw is both the reality of the resurrection and the gentleness of the Saviour. The young man gave unmistakable proof that life had returned: he "sat up," the very posture of the living, and "began to speak," showing mind and voice restored — no swoon or half-recovery, but full and certain life. The witnesses note Luke's medical precision in the word for "sat up," the physician marking the soundness of the cure. But the most affecting touch, the expositors press, is the last: Christ "delivered him to his mother." He did not merely raise the dead to display his power, but restored the son to the widow's arms, meeting the very grief that had moved his compassion. Ellicott's reading, the witnesses observe, lends it depth: the mother received her son a second time, "God-given" now in a higher sense than at his birth — a gift from the Lord of life himself. Bengel's note adds the poignancy: the son had ceased to belong to her, claimed by death, and Christ gave him back. The verse thus completes the miracle with mercy: the dead raised, life proved, and the son restored to the weeping widow — the power of Christ serving the tenderness of his compassion.
16:
"And there came a fear on all: and they glorified God, saying, That a great prophet is risen up among us; and, That God hath visited his people." The witnesses note the awe and praise of the crowd. The Reformed Gill distinguishes the fear: "not a fear of dread and terror... but a fear and reverence of the divine majesty, whose power and presence they were sensible must be there." Barnes describes "an awe or solemnity at the presence of one who had power to raise the dead," and notes the people "glorified God that he had sent such a prophet" and believed "God hath visited his people" (Luke 1:68). Barnes stresses the certainty of the miracle: "there was no doubt that he was dead... he met the funeral procession as it were by accident, and by a word restored him to life; the evidence was decisive."

The witnesses note the two confessions: "a great prophet is risen up" and "God hath visited his people" — the crowd recognizing in the miracle the marks of God's prophetic visitation, though not yet the full truth of who Christ was.

So the witnesses make the verse the response of the awed multitude. The lesson the expositors draw is the holy fear and the praise that the miracle awakened. The crowd was seized with "fear" — not the terror of the guilty, the witnesses note, but the reverent awe that the presence of divine power and majesty rightly produces; they felt that God himself was near. And out of this awe they "glorified God," ascribing the wonder to him: a great prophet had risen among them, and "God hath visited his people," fulfilling the language of Zacharias's song (Luke 1:68). The witnesses note that the people's confession, true as far as it went, fell short of the full truth — they saw a prophet through whom God worked, not yet the Lord of life himself; yet their instinct was right, that God had drawn near in mercy. Barnes's emphasis, the expositors press, magnifies the evidential force of this miracle: the man was undoubtedly dead, the meeting unplanned, the restoration by a word, the witnesses many — a miracle as public and undeniable as could be, leaving no room for collusion or delusion. The verse thus shows the crowd rightly awed and praising God, recognizing his visitation, even as they had not yet grasped that the One who raised the dead was God visiting his people in person.
17:
"And this rumour of him went forth throughout all Judaea, and throughout all the region round about." The witnesses note the spreading report of the miracle and its providential purpose. Cyril of Alexandria presses the reason Luke records so undeniable a raising: "He joins miracle to miracle... lest some one might rise up and say, in opposition to the Saviour's glory, What is the prodigy wrought in the case of the centurion's son? for though he was ailing, he was in no danger of death... To stop the intemperate tongue of such detractors, he says that Christ met the dead young man, the only son of a widow." The witnesses note Nain was in Galilee, yet the fame spread even to Judaea and all the surrounding region.

The report, the witnesses agree, carried the news of this public and undeniable miracle far and wide, preparing the way for the inquiry of John's disciples that follows, and for the wider testimony to Christ's works.

So the witnesses make the verse the spreading of Christ's fame and the link to what follows. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that published abroad so decisive a proof of Christ's power. The raising of the widow's son, the witnesses note, was no hidden act but a public wonder, witnessed by two great crowds; and its report went out through all Judaea and the surrounding country, so that the fame of Christ as one who raised the dead reached far beyond Nain. Cyril's observation, the expositors press, shows the evidential design: Luke records this certain raising — of a man undoubtedly dead, carried to burial — precisely to silence any cavil that the earlier miracle (the centurion's servant) was no true display of power over death, since the servant might have recovered anyway. Here there could be no such objection: the man was dead, and Christ raised him before many witnesses. The wide spread of the rumour, the witnesses note, also serves the narrative's progress: it was doubtless among the things reported to John in prison, prompting the inquiry of his disciples that the next verses record. The verse thus marks the growing fame of Christ, the undeniable evidence of his power over death now noised through the whole land, and sets the stage for John's question and Christ's great testimony concerning his works.
18:
"And the disciples of John shewed him of all these things." The witnesses note how the report reached the imprisoned Baptist and debate the spirit of his disciples. Cyril notes that "certain of His disciples relate to the holy Baptist the miracle which was known to all." Bede reads their motive less charitably: "not, as it seems to me, in simpleness of heart, but provoked by envy; for in another place they complain, Rabbi, he that was with you beyond Jordan, behold the same baptizes, and all men come to him" (John 3:26). Chrysostom finds in John's response a pastoral wisdom: "we are then most raised up to Him when we are fallen into straits; John therefore, being cast into prison, takes the opportunity, when his disciples were most in need of Jesus, to send them to Christ."

The witnesses note John was now imprisoned by Herod, and his disciples brought him word of Christ's mighty works — the occasion of the question he would send.

So the witnesses make the verse the prelude to John's inquiry. The lesson the expositors draw is the providential occasion by which John would direct his disciples to Christ. The Baptist, now shut in Herod's prison, heard from his own disciples of "all these things" — the raising at Nain and the other wonders Christ wrought. The witnesses note the fathers' suspicion that John's disciples reported these things "provoked by envy," jealous (as elsewhere) for their master's honour against the growing fame of Jesus — a jealousy John himself had rebuked, declaring "He must increase, but I must decrease" (John 3:30). Yet, the expositors press, John turned their report to good: knowing his own end was near and his disciples needed to be weaned from him to Christ, he seized the moment, when they were most stirred by news of Jesus, to send them to the Lord himself. Chrysostom's insight, the witnesses observe, frames the whole: John acts not from his own doubt but from concern for his disciples, using his imprisonment and their unsettled state to direct them where they must finally look. The verse thus sets the scene — the imprisoned forerunner, his disciples bringing news of Christ — and prepares for the question that would draw from Christ both an answer to John's followers and a great testimony to the Baptist himself.
19:
"And John calling unto him two of his disciples sent them to Jesus, saying, Art thou he that should come? or look we for another?" The witnesses address the great question: did John himself doubt? Matthew Henry frames the inquiry: "the great thing we are to enquire concerning Christ is whether he be he that should come to redeem and save sinners, or whether we are to look for another." He holds the doubt was the disciples', not John's settled mind: "the faith of John Baptist himself, or at least of his disciples, wanted to be confirmed in this matter; for Christ had not yet publicly declared himself to be the Christ." Gill notes "two of his disciples" were "a sufficient number to be sent on an errand and bear witness."

The phrase "he that should come" (better "the coming One"), the witnesses note, was a recognized title of the Messiah; John asks whether Jesus is indeed that promised one, or whether another is still to be awaited.

So the witnesses make the verse the famous inquiry and the occasion of much debate. The lesson the expositors draw turns on the spirit of the question. The dominant reading, the witnesses note, is that the doubt was not John's own — for he had borne witness to Jesus as the Lamb of God and the Son of God — but his disciples', whose faith needed confirming; John sent them to Christ that they might see and hear for themselves, and so be established. Henry presses this: John knew who Christ was, but his followers, jealous and unsettled, needed to have their faith strengthened by Christ's own works and words, since Jesus had not yet openly proclaimed himself Messiah. A minority of the fathers, the witnesses observe, allowed that John or his disciples genuinely wavered under the strain of imprisonment. But the great body of the witnesses hold that the question was sent for the disciples' sake, to direct them from John to Christ. The title "the coming One," the expositors note, is the Messianic name; John asks whether Jesus is the promised Redeemer or whether expectation must still look further. The verse thus poses the central question of the gospel — is Jesus the Christ? — and sends John's disciples to find the answer in Christ's own mighty works.
20:
"When the men were come unto him, they said, John Baptist hath sent us unto thee, saying, Art thou he that should come? or look we for another?" The witnesses note the faithful delivery of the message and one father's striking application. The grammatical-historical Gill identifies the men as "the same that John sent from the castle of Machaerus, where he was now a prisoner, to Christ." Ellicott confirms "he that should come" is "the coming One." The disciples repeat John's question exactly, as faithful messengers.

Cyril of Jerusalem uses the verse to teach the descent of Christ to the dead: among those Christ went down to redeem was "John himself also, who by his messengers said, Art thou He that should come, or look we for another?" — the herald redeemed by the King he had announced. The witnesses note this is a homiletical application, not the verse's plain sense.

So the witnesses make the verse the delivery of John's inquiry to Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the fidelity of the messengers and the directness of the question now laid before Jesus. The two disciples, sent from John's prison at Machaerus, came to Christ and repeated their master's words exactly: "Art thou he that should come? or look we for another?" The witnesses note the plain faithfulness of the report — they added nothing and took nothing away, but set the question squarely before the Lord, that his answer might be brought back to John and his followers. Cyril of Jerusalem's use of the verse, the expositors observe, is a devout application beyond the literal sense: in expounding Christ's descent to the realm of the dead to redeem the righteous of old, he lists John among them — the forerunner who had announced Christ to the living now awaiting redemption by the same Christ among the dead. This, the witnesses note, is preaching from the text, not its primary meaning; the verse itself simply records the disciples' delivery of John's question. The verse thus brings the inquiry to its point: the messengers stand before Christ with John's question, and the next verses give his answer — first in deeds, then in words — to which they were to bear witness back to the imprisoned Baptist.
21:
"And in that same hour he cured many of their infirmities and plagues, and of evil spirits; and unto many that were blind he gave sight." The witnesses note that Christ answered first by works, and a distinction Luke the physician draws. The grammatical-historical Clarke cites Bishop Pearce on Luke's precise vocabulary: "Luke mentions diseases or ill habits of body, sores or lamenesses, and evil spirits — from whence we may conclude that evil spirits are reckoned by him (who speaks of distempers with more accuracy than the other evangelists) as things different from any disorders of the body." On the blind, Clarke renders "he kindly gave sight" — "he graciously gave sight," noting "in all his miracles Jesus showed the tenderest mercy." Ellicott observes the statement is "peculiar to St. Luke, and obviously adds much force to our Lord's answer: He pointed to what was passing before the eyes of the questioners."

Christ's reply, the witnesses note, was first a display: in the very hour the messengers came, he wrought a multitude of cures, that they might report what they had seen.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's answer in deed before his answer in word. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ proved his Messiahship by his works, set before the eyes of John's disciples. Rather than reply at once with a bare claim, the witnesses note, Christ "in that same hour" healed many — diseases, sores, demoniacs, the blind — so that the messengers should have firsthand evidence to carry back. Ellicott's observation, the expositors press, is key: the force of Christ's verbal answer (in the next verse) depends on this — he could bid them tell John "what they had seen," because they had just seen the very works the prophets foretold of the Messiah. Clarke's note on Luke's medical precision, the witnesses observe, is characteristic of the physician-evangelist, who carefully distinguishes bodily diseases from demonic possession, treating the latter as a distinct reality, not mere illness. And Clarke's rendering "graciously gave sight," the expositors note, captures the tenderness with which Christ wrought even these evidential miracles — mercy and proof joined. The verse thus shows Christ answering John's question by doing the works of the Messiah before the messengers' eyes, that their report might rest not on his word only but on what they had witnessed.
22:
"Then Jesus answering said unto them, Go your way, and tell John what things ye have seen and heard; how that the blind see, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, to the poor the gospel is preached." The witnesses note that Christ's answer points to the Messianic prophecies fulfilled. The grammatical-historical Barnes lists the cross-references: the blind seeing, the lame walking (Isaiah 35:5-6), and "to the poor the gospel is preached" (Luke 4:18; cf. Isaiah 61:1). Robertson notes Luke's "what things ye have seen and heard" (where Matthew has the present "ye do hear and see"), and that Luke's earlier verse gives the specific cures. Wesley singles out the climax: "to the poor the gospel is preached — which is the greatest mercy, and the greatest miracle of all."

Christ's answer, the witnesses note, is no direct "I am he," but a pointing to his works, which were the very signs the prophets had assigned to the Messianic age — so that John's disciples might draw the conclusion themselves.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's Messianic self-attestation by his works. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ answers the question "Art thou he?" by displaying the credentials the prophets had foretold of the Messiah. He bids the messengers "tell John what ye have seen and heard" — and the catalogue, the witnesses note, is drawn straight from Isaiah's portrait of the days of salvation: the blind see, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised (Isaiah 35:5-6). These works, the expositors press, were the recognized signs of the Messiah's coming; by pointing to them, Christ answers John's question more powerfully than by a bare claim, for he lets the prophecies and their fulfilment speak. The climax, the witnesses note with Wesley, is the last clause: "to the poor the gospel is preached" — the greatest mercy and the greatest miracle of all, for the raising of the dead is less wonderful than the bringing of good news to the poor and lost. The Messiah is known, the expositors observe, not only by power over disease and death but by grace to the poor in spirit. The verse thus gives Christ's answer to John: the works of the promised Messiah are being done, and the gospel is preached to the poor; let John and his disciples judge from these whether Jesus is "he that should come."
23:
"And blessed is he, whosoever shall not be offended in me." The witnesses note this gentle word, aimed especially at John's disciples. The grammatical-historical Gill observes the versions expand it — "blessed is he that doubts not of me," "blessed is he that is not brought into offence and doubt concerning me" — and notes "particular regard is had to the disciples of John, who both doubted of Christ as the Messiah, and were offended at his popularity and success." Barnes cross-references the stone of stumbling (Isaiah 8:14-15; Luke 2:34; John 6:66) over which men fall.

The beatitude, the witnesses agree, is both a warning and a comfort: blessed is he who, despite the lowliness of Christ's appearance and the offence of his ways, does not stumble at him but believes.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's gentle appeal not to be offended in him. The lesson the expositors draw is the blessedness of a faith that is not scandalized by the lowly form of the Messiah. Having pointed to his works, Christ adds this word — "blessed is he, whosoever shall not be offended in me" — aimed, the witnesses note, particularly at John's disciples, who were tempted to stumble: they had expected a different Messiah, were troubled by the lowliness and the manner of Jesus, and perhaps offended at his eclipsing of their master. Christ does not rebuke them harshly, but pronounces a blessing on those who do not take offence — a tender invitation to believe despite the things that perplexed them. The witnesses note the deeper truth: Christ was "set for the fall and rising of many," a stone of stumbling (Isaiah 8:14; Luke 2:34), over which the proud and unbelieving fall, but on which the humble are built; and the blessing is for those who, finding in him nothing to match their carnal expectations of a triumphant Messiah, yet believe. The expositors press the abiding application: in every age men are tempted to be offended at Christ — at his lowliness, his cross, his demands — and the blessing rests on those who, like the believing, are not scandalized but cleave to him. The verse thus closes Christ's answer to John with a beatitude that is also a warning: happy are they who do not stumble at the Christ of God.
24:
"And when the messengers of John were departed, he began to speak unto the people concerning John, What went ye out into the wilderness for to see? A reed shaken with the wind?" The witnesses note that Christ waited for the messengers to leave before praising John, and the meaning of the "reed." Cyril explains the purpose: "the Lord, knowing the secrets of men, foresaw that some would say, If until now John is ignorant of Jesus, how did he show Him to us, saying, Behold the Lamb of God? To quench this feeling... He began to speak concerning John." The Greek expositor notes the timing: "these things were spoken after the departure of John's disciples, for He would not utter the praises of the Baptist while they were present, lest His words should be counted as those of a flatterer."

On "a reed shaken with the wind," Cyril paraphrases: "you marvelled at John, and came to see him, passing over long journeys in the desert; surely in vain, if you think him so fickle as to be like a reed bending whichever way the wind moves it" — such a one being he "who lightly avows his ignorance of what he knows."

So the witnesses make the verse the beginning of Christ's testimony to John. The lesson the expositors draw is the vindication of John's steadfastness and the delicacy of Christ's praise. As soon as the messengers had gone, the witnesses note, Christ turned to commend John to the people — waiting, the Greek expositor observes, until the disciples were absent, lest his praise seem flattery or be misread as encouraging John's followers in their attachment. And his first word defends John's constancy: "What went ye out to see? A reed shaken with the wind?" — that is, did you go into the desert to see a weak, wavering man, fickle as a reed bent by every breeze? The implied answer, the expositors press, is no: John was no such waverer, and his question to Christ (whatever it seemed) was not the sign of an unstable mind. Cyril's reading, the witnesses note, ties this to the question just sent: lest any think John doubted what he had once confessed, Christ affirms his firmness — John was no reed, but a man of unshaken conviction. The verse thus opens Christ's great testimony to the Baptist, beginning by clearing him of the charge of fickleness, and preparing to declare him more than a prophet, the greatest born of women.
25:
"But what went ye out for to see? A man clothed in soft raiment? Behold, they which are gorgeously apparelled, and live delicately, are in kings' courts." The witnesses note the second question and its sharp social point. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks Luke's vivid words: "more vivid than those in St. Matthew (soft clothing), bringing out the idea of ostentatious display and extravagant excess of luxury, as well as effeminate self-indulgence." He notes "such forms of selfishness were common among the house of Herod and their followers," and traces "a touch of brutal vindictiveness in the gorgeous robe in which they arrayed the Prophet of Nazareth" (Luke 23:11). Robertson glosses "live delicately" (tryphē, "luxurious living") and "in kings' courts."

The point, the witnesses agree, is that John was no soft courtier seeking ease and finery; the desert and the camel's-hair garment marked him as the opposite of the luxurious flatterers who haunt kings' palaces.

So the witnesses make the verse the second stage of Christ's testimony to John's character. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between John's austere integrity and the soft self-indulgence of the world. Christ asks again: did you go into the wilderness to see "a man clothed in soft raiment"? — the implied answer again being no, for such men, the witnesses note, are found not in deserts but "in kings' courts," gorgeously dressed and living in luxury. John was the very opposite: a man of the wilderness, clad in camel's hair, fed on locusts and wild honey, who feared no king and flattered none — indeed, who would lose his head for rebuking a king's sin. Ellicott's observation, the expositors press, sharpens the point with bitter irony: the luxurious courtiers of Herod's house, against whom John's austerity stood as a rebuke, were the very men who would array the lowly Christ in a "gorgeous robe" of mockery. The witnesses note the implied censure of the soft and self-indulgent: the kingdom of God is heralded not by the pampered courtier but by the rugged prophet who lives plainly and speaks boldly. The verse thus continues Christ's vindication of John — no fickle reed, no soft courtier — building toward the climactic declaration that John was a prophet, and more than a prophet, the messenger sent before the Lord.
26:
"But what went ye out for to see? A prophet? Yea, I say unto you, and much more than a prophet." The witnesses note the rising climax of Christ's testimony. The grammatical-historical Robertson expounds the prophet's office: "a real prophet will always get a hearing if he has a message from God; he is a forth-teller... The word of God came to John in the wilderness (Luke 3:2); that made him a prophet." Barnes cross-references the angel's word that John would be "the prophet of the Highest" (Luke 1:76). But Christ declares him "much more than a prophet" — greater than the prophetic line that went before.

John was more than a prophet, the witnesses note, because the prophets foretold the Messiah from afar, but John pointed him out present — "Behold the Lamb of God" — and was himself the subject of prophecy, the forerunner sent immediately before the Lord.

So the witnesses make the verse the height of Christ's praise of John. The lesson the expositors draw is John's unique place among the prophets — a prophet, and more. Christ asks a third time, "What went ye out to see? A prophet?" — and now answers his own question: yes, a prophet, "and much more than a prophet." The witnesses note what made John a prophet: the word of God came to him, and he spoke it boldly, which is the essence of the prophetic office — to have and to declare a message from God, whatever the cost. But John was "much more," the expositors press, for two reasons the witnesses draw out: the prophets before him had foretold the Messiah from a distance, but John saw him, pointed to him, and baptized him; and John was himself the subject of prophecy, the promised messenger sent to prepare the way (as the next verse declares). He stood, that is, at the very threshold between the old covenant and the new, the last and greatest of the prophets, who not only predicted but introduced the Christ. The witnesses note the dignity Christ thus confers on the imprisoned Baptist, vindicating him before the people as no waverer, no courtier, but the greatest of the prophetic line. The verse thus lifts John above all who went before, and prepares for the declaration that he is the very messenger of Malachi's prophecy, sent before the face of the Lord.
27:
"This is he, of whom it is written, Behold, I send my messenger before thy face, which shall prepare thy way before thee." The witnesses note that Christ applies Malachi's prophecy to John. The grammatical-historical Gill and Wesley refer it to Malachi 3:1 — "Behold, I will send my messenger, and he shall prepare the way before me." Barnes cross-references the angel's announcement that John would go before the Lord in the spirit and power of Elijah (Luke 1:15-17). The prophecy, the witnesses note, marks John as the forerunner divinely appointed to go immediately before the Messiah.

The application, the witnesses agree, is what makes John "more than a prophet": he is the very messenger whom Scripture foretold, sent before the face of the Lord to prepare his way — a dignity no earlier prophet shared.

So the witnesses make the verse the scriptural ground of John's unique greatness. The lesson the expositors draw is that John fulfils the prophecy of the forerunner, and so stands in a place above all the prophets. Christ declares, "This is he, of whom it is written" — and cites Malachi's word concerning the messenger sent before the Lord to prepare his way. The witnesses note the weight of this: John is not merely a prophet who spoke of the Messiah, but the very subject of prophecy, the herald whose coming was foretold as the immediate prelude to the Lord's own appearing. This, the expositors press, is why he is "much more than a prophet": the others looked forward to a distant day, but John was himself the predicted forerunner, standing at the door of the Messianic age, preparing the way of the Lord by his preaching of repentance and his baptism. The application of Malachi 3:1, the witnesses observe, also carries a profound implication for Christ himself: the prophecy speaks of preparing the way "before me," that is, before the Lord God — and Christ applies the "thy way" and "thy face" to himself, so that he is the Lord whose way John prepared. The verse thus crowns Christ's testimony to John by setting him in the place of the prophesied messenger, and, in so doing, quietly claims for Christ the dignity of the Lord whom that messenger went before.
28:
"For I say unto you, Among those that are born of women there is not a greater prophet than John the Baptist: but he that is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he." The witnesses note the height of John's greatness and the paradox of the kingdom. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes Matthew's report is "somewhat more emphatic, there has not been raised up," and Robertson glosses "there is none — no one exists." Cyril of Jerusalem presses the comparison: "Baptism is the end of the Old Testament and beginning of the New; its author was John, than whom was none greater among them that are born of women... You may mention Elias, taken up into heaven, yet he is not greater than John; Enoch was translated, but not greater; Moses was a great lawgiver, but not greater — their Master and ours, the Lord Jesus, declared it."

Yet "he that is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he" — the witnesses note the paradox: the humblest member of the gospel kingdom, knowing Christ crucified and risen, has a privilege beyond John, who stood at the threshold but did not see the full glory.

So the witnesses make the verse the climax of John's praise and the measure of the kingdom's privilege. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the unsurpassed greatness of John: of all born of women — Christ being the standard, John exceeds all merely human greatness — none was greater. Cyril's catalogue, the witnesses note, presses the point: not Elijah, not Enoch, not Moses, great as they were, surpassed John; for he stood at the hinge of the ages, the last of the old and the herald of the new, the one who baptized the Lord and pointed him out. Second, the greater privilege of the least in the kingdom: "he that is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he." The witnesses note this is not a comparison of personal holiness but of position and privilege — the humblest believer under the gospel, who knows the accomplished work of Christ, his death, resurrection, and the outpoured Spirit, enjoys a clearer light and fuller blessing than John, who, great as he was, stood only at the door and died before the kingdom came in power. The expositors press the wonder: the meanest Christian, in point of privilege and knowledge of the finished redemption, is more favoured than the greatest of the prophets. The verse thus honours John to the height while exalting the surpassing grace of the gospel kingdom, in which the least see what prophets and kings desired to see and did not.
29:
"And all the people that heard him, and the publicans, justified God, being baptized with the baptism of John." The witnesses note the response of the common people and a rich sense of "justified God." Chrysostom sets the contrast: "having declared the praises of John, he next exposes the great fault of the Pharisees and lawyers, who would not after the publicans receive the baptism of John." Ambrose expounds "justified God": "God is justified by baptism, wherein men justify themselves confessing their sins; for he that sins and confesses his sin to God justifies God, submitting himself to Him who overcomes, and hoping for grace." Eusebius adds: "because they believed, they justified God, for He appeared just to them in all that He did."

The witnesses note the striking reversal: the despised publicans and common people, by receiving John's baptism and confessing their sins, "justified God" — acknowledged his righteousness and submitted to his way — while the religious leaders did not.

So the witnesses make the verse the vindication of God by the humble. The lesson the expositors draw is the meaning of "justifying God" and the reversal it reveals. The common people and the publicans — the very sinners the Pharisees despised — had received John's baptism, confessing their sins; and in so doing, the witnesses note, they "justified God." This phrase, the expositors press, does not mean they made God righteous (he is righteous in himself), but that they acknowledged and declared his righteousness — owning that his call to repentance was just, submitting to his way of salvation, and confessing their own sin. Ambrose's reading deepens it: in confessing sin, men justify God, taking his side against themselves, owning him just and themselves guilty, and so hoping for grace. The witnesses note the pointed contrast Chrysostom draws: the despised publicans submitted to God's righteousness, while the proud leaders (in the next verse) rejected it. The verse thus shows where true wisdom and humility were found — not among the learned and self-righteous, but among the humble who confessed their sin and received the baptism of repentance. They "justified God," declaring his dealings right and themselves wrong; and in that humble confession lay the beginning of their salvation. The verse thus prepares the contrast with the Pharisees and lawyers, who, refusing John's baptism, rejected the very counsel of God for their own destruction.
30:
"But the Pharisees and lawyers rejected the counsel of God against themselves, being not baptized of him." The witnesses note the tragic refusal of the leaders and the meaning of "the counsel of God." The Reformed Gill clarifies: "by the counsel of God here is not meant his purpose, intention, and design, which is never frustrated; but the precept of God" — the command to repent and be baptized; they "rejected the command of God unto them." Barnes notes that though some Pharisees came to John (Matthew 3:7), "the great mass of Pharisees and lawyers rejected him," and "the counsel of God toward them was the solemn admonition by John to repent and be baptized." Bede draws the self-harm: "he who rejects the grace of God does it against himself."

The witnesses note the phrase "against themselves" — the rejection was to their own ruin, bringing destruction, temporal and eternal, upon those who refused the way of repentance.

So the witnesses make the verse the dark counterpart to the people's faith. The lesson the expositors draw is the self-destruction of those who reject God's gracious call. The Pharisees and lawyers — the learned, the religious, the leaders of Israel — "rejected the counsel of God," refusing John's baptism of repentance. The witnesses note Gill's careful distinction: "the counsel of God" here is not God's secret purpose (which none can frustrate) but his revealed command and gracious provision — the call to repent and be baptized, to prepare for the Messiah. This they spurned, the expositors press, and so rejected the very means God had appointed for their salvation. And they did it "against themselves," Bede notes — for to refuse the grace of God is to wound none but oneself, bringing ruin on one's own soul. The witnesses observe the searching contrast with the foregoing verse: the despised publicans, by submitting to baptism, justified God and found mercy; the honoured leaders, by refusing it, condemned themselves. The expositors draw the abiding warning: the rejection of God's call is not a neutral act but a self-inflicted ruin; those who, in pride, will not own their sin and receive God's appointed grace, shut themselves out of the kingdom. The verse thus exposes the fatal unbelief of the leaders, who, having every advantage, rejected the counsel of God to their own destruction.
31:
"And the Lord said, Whereunto then shall I liken the men of this generation? and to what are they like?" The witnesses note a textual point and the rhetorical force. The grammatical-historical Gill, Clarke, and Robertson observe that "And the Lord said" is omitted in many manuscripts and ancient versions — Clarke notes the lectionaries (Evangelistaria) began here, and "the words were probably at first used by them to introduce the following parable... every critic rejects them." Its omission, Gill notes, "more clearly shows that the two former verses are the words of Christ," not the evangelist's comment. Robertson notes Luke's added second question, "to what are they like?", "sharpens the point," not in Matthew.

The double question, the witnesses agree, introduces the parable of the children in the marketplace, by which Christ would expose the perverse unbelief of his generation.

So the witnesses make the verse the introduction to Christ's parable of the petulant children. The lesson the expositors draw is the deliberate, searching way Christ exposes the unreasonable unbelief of his contemporaries. The doubled question — "Whereunto shall I liken the men of this generation? and to what are they like?" — the witnesses note (with Robertson), sharpens the point and arrests attention, preparing for the apt and stinging comparison to follow. The textual note, the expositors observe, is of some interest: the clause "And the Lord said" is likely a later liturgical addition, marking where the public reading began, and its absence shows that verses 29-30 (on the people and the Pharisees) are Christ's own continued speech, not Luke's editorial aside. But the substance is plain: Christ, having praised John and exposed the leaders' rejection, now turns to characterize the whole perverse generation that received neither John nor himself. The witnesses note the gravity of the question: Christ seeks a fitting image for a people so contradictory in their unbelief that nothing could please them — neither John's austerity nor Christ's freedom. The verse thus opens the parable that follows, in which the men of that generation are likened to froward children who will neither dance to the pipe nor mourn to the dirge — the very picture of a captious unbelief that rejects every overture of God.
32:
"They are like unto children sitting in the marketplace, and calling one to another, and saying, We have piped unto you, and ye have not danced; we have mourned to you, and ye have not wept." The witnesses expound the parable of the petulant children. The Reformed Gill explains the likeness: the Pharisees "are like to children, not for innocence... but for their ignorance, folly, and weakness," and specifically "to those surly and ill-natured ones who made no answer to those that did" — the froward children who would join in no game. Robertson notes the funeral allusion: "ye did not mourn (or beat your breast); they all did it at funerals; these children would not play wedding or funeral." Wesley sums it up: "so froward and perverse that no contrivance can be found to please them."

The picture, the witnesses agree, is of children in the marketplace who reject every game their fellows propose — neither the merry piping (a wedding) nor the sad mourning (a funeral) — the very image of a captious people whom nothing can satisfy.

So the witnesses make the verse the heart of Christ's parable against his unbelieving generation. The lesson the expositors draw is the perverse and contradictory unbelief that rejects every approach of God. The men of that generation, the witnesses note, are likened to peevish children who will play no game: when their fellows pipe a merry tune, they will not dance; when they sing a dirge, they will not mourn. Nothing pleases them, the expositors press — they find fault with every overture. So it was with the Jews toward John and Christ: John came in austerity, fasting and mourning, and they rejected him as too severe; Christ came in freedom, eating and drinking, and they rejected him as too lax. The witnesses note the bitter aptness: the fault lay not in the messengers but in the hearers, whose unbelief was determined to be displeased whatever God sent. Wesley's summary, the expositors observe, captures it: "no contrivance can be found to please them," for the root was not in the manner of the message but in the hardness of the heart. The verse thus exposes the irrationality of unbelief, which, like a sulky child, rejects both the joyful and the solemn, both grace and warning; and it prepares for the application that follows, in which John's mourning and Christ's freedom are alike spurned by a generation resolved not to be won.
33:
"For John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine; and ye say, He hath a devil." The witnesses apply the parable to John's austerity. The Reformed Gill identifies John "with the children that mourned... he preached mournful doctrine, delivered it in a solemn and awful manner, lived a very austere life, and fasted much." Robertson notes Luke alone adds "bread" and "wine," specifying the abstinence. The charge "he hath a devil," the witnesses note, was the people's slander against John's severe, ascetic life — they attributed his fasting and solitude to demonic influence rather than holiness.

John, the witnesses agree, answered to the mourning children: he came in fasting and austerity, calling to repentance with solemn warning; and the generation rejected him, calling his holiness madness or possession.

So the witnesses make the verse the first half of the application. The lesson the expositors draw is the unbelief that slanders even the holiest austerity. John, the witnesses note, came in the way of the mourning children — fasting, abstaining from bread and wine, living the severe life of a Nazarite in the wilderness, preaching repentance with solemn weight. And how did the generation respond? They said, "He hath a devil" — attributing his rigour and his solitary, abstemious life not to the power of the Spirit but to demonic possession, as though such severity could only come from a deranged or possessed mind. The witnesses note the perversity: the very holiness and self-denial that should have commanded reverence were turned into a reproach; men who loved their ease and pleasure could not believe that genuine devotion lay behind such austerity, and so dismissed it as madness. Robertson's note on Luke's specifying "bread and wine," the expositors observe, underscores how complete John's abstinence was. The verse thus shows the first way the captious generation rejected God's overture: when he sent the mourning prophet, calling to repentance in austerity, they scorned him as devil-possessed. And the next verse will show the other half: when God sent his Son in freedom and grace, they scorned him too — proving that the fault was never in the message but in the unbelieving heart that would be pleased by nothing.
34:
"The Son of man is come eating and drinking; and ye say, Behold a gluttonous man, and a winebibber, a friend of publicans and sinners!" The witnesses apply the other half of the parable to Christ's freedom. The Reformed Gill notes Christ "is come eating and drinking, as other people do, and shuns no man's company, goes to a wedding, dines with a Pharisee, eats with publicans and sinners, and carries it freely and courteously to all." Calvin draws the lesson against false asceticism: "those who think the highest perfection consists in outward austerity, and who pronounce it an angelical life when a person is abstemious or mortifies himself by fasting, ought to attend to this passage; on this principle John would rank higher than the Son of God" — but "bodily exercise profiteth little, but godliness is profitable to all things" (1 Timothy 4:8). Robertson notes that even today some pervert "friend of sinners" into a slur on Christ's character.

Christ, the witnesses note, answered to the piping children: he came in social freedom, eating and drinking, mingling with all; and the generation slandered him as a glutton and drunkard, a friend of the disreputable.

So the witnesses make the verse the second half of the application. The lesson the expositors draw is the unbelief that slanders even gracious freedom, and the warning against false notions of holiness. Christ, the witnesses note, came in the opposite manner to John: not in austere fasting but in social freedom — eating and drinking, attending feasts, dining with Pharisees and publicans alike, courteous and accessible to all. And the generation slandered him as readily as they had slandered John, but in the opposite terms: "a gluttonous man, and a winebibber, a friend of publicans and sinners." The witnesses note the same perverse unbelief at work: when God sent austerity, they cried "devil"; when he sent freedom, they cried "glutton" — proving the fault lay not in either manner but in their hardened hearts. Calvin's reflection, the expositors press, draws a doctrinal lesson: the passage rebukes those who measure holiness by mere outward severity, who would rank the abstemious John above the freely-living Son of God; true godliness is not in abstinence but in the heart, and Christ's free manner of life was no less holy than John's austerity. The witnesses note the precious title hidden in the slander: "a friend of publicans and sinners" — meant as reproach, but in truth the glory of the Saviour who came to seek and save the lost. The verse thus completes the parable: both John and Christ, austerity and freedom, were alike rejected by a generation that would be pleased by nothing.
35:
"But wisdom is justified of all her children." The witnesses expound this concluding proverb. The Reformed Gill takes "wisdom" as Christ himself: "Christ, who is the wisdom of God, and who acted the wise part in behaving in such a free manner with all sorts of men... whereby he became useful to their souls"; and "her children" are those given him by the Father, who "justify him from all such scandalous imputations, and by their lives show that the doctrine of Christ is not a licentious one." He notes Luke's added "all" (not in Matthew) "signifying that this is the universal sense and practice of all the real offspring of Christ."
