The proverb, the witnesses note, declares that despite the slanders of the unbelieving generation, the wisdom of God — in John's austerity and Christ's freedom alike — is vindicated by those who are truly wise, the children of wisdom, who receive and prove it.

So the witnesses make the verse the vindication of God's wisdom against the cavils of unbelief. The lesson the expositors draw is that the wisdom of God in his dealings is justified by those who are its true children, however the perverse generation scorns it. The men of that generation, the witnesses note, had slandered both John and Christ, finding fault with austerity and freedom alike; but their unbelief could not overturn the wisdom of God. That wisdom — whether in sending John to mourn or the Son to pipe — "is justified of all her children": acknowledged, approved, and vindicated by those who are truly wise, the spiritual offspring of wisdom, who receive both messengers and profit by both. Gill's reading, the witnesses note, identifies the Wisdom as Christ himself, the wisdom of God incarnate, whose free and gracious manner of life, far from being licentious, was the very wisdom that won and saved sinners; and his children — those given him by the Father, born again, walking in holiness — justify him, proving by their changed lives that his doctrine leads not to license but to godliness. Luke's "all," the expositors observe, presses the universality: every true child of wisdom, without exception, owns and vindicates the wisdom of God's ways. The verse thus closes the passage on a note of vindication: the unbelieving may slander, but the wise receive; and the wisdom of God, in all its varied dealings, stands justified by all who are truly its children.
36:
"And one of the Pharisees desired him that he would eat with him. And he went into the Pharisee's house, and sat down to meat." The witnesses introduce the anointing at Simon's house and the grace of Christ's acceptance. Bede ties it to the foregoing: "having said, the people justified God, the Evangelist builds up in deed what he proposed in word, namely, wisdom justified by the righteous and the penitent." Gregory of Nyssa rebukes the separatist spirit: "there are very many who justify themselves, puffed up with idle fancy, who before the Judgment separate themselves as lambs from the herds, not willing even to eat with the many." Against this, "St. Luke, the physician of souls, represents our Lord most mercifully visiting others" — not to share their faults, "but to impart somewhat of His own righteousness."

The witnesses note Christ's gracious freedom: he accepts the Pharisee's invitation and reclines at his table, as he had eaten with publicans — a living illustration of the freedom just defended (v.34), and an occasion for the great lesson on love and forgiveness to follow.

So the witnesses make the verse the setting of the anointing and a lesson in Christ's gracious accessibility. The lesson the expositors draw is the condescension of Christ, who ate with Pharisee and publican alike, that he might do good to all. Bede's note, the witnesses observe, links the scene to the parable just told: Christ, having shown himself a friend of sinners, now proves it in deed — both eating with this Pharisee and receiving the penitent sinner who comes to him, so that wisdom is justified by the righteous and the penitent together. Gregory of Nyssa's rebuke, the expositors press, has abiding force: against the self-righteous who separate themselves, refusing even to eat with others, Christ shows the better way — the physician who visits the sick not to catch their disease but to heal it, mingling with men not to share their sin but to impart his righteousness. The witnesses note the irony soon to unfold: the Pharisee who invited Christ would prove the less loving host, while the despised sinner would show the greater devotion. The verse thus opens the scene with Christ at the Pharisee's table — accessible, gracious, free — and prepares for the entrance of the weeping woman, whose love would expose the cold heart of the host and draw from Christ the parable of the two debtors and the word of pardon.
37:
"And, behold, a woman in the city, which was a sinner, when she knew that Jesus sat at meat in the Pharisee's house, brought an alabaster box of ointment." The witnesses address the woman's identity and her notorious character. Gill argues she is "not Mary Magdalene... nor Mary the sister of Lazarus," and that this anointing in Galilee, in Simon the Pharisee's house, is a distinct event from the later anointing at Bethany. Lightfoot explains "a sinner" in the Jewish sense: "women of an ill name," those who transgressed the law of Moses and Jewish custom — generally, a woman of immoral life. Bede notes the alabaster: "a kind of white marble... generally used for vessels holding ointment, because it is the best sort for preserving the ointment sweet."

The witnesses note the woman's faith and resolve: knowing where Christ was, she came uninvited into the Pharisee's house, bringing her precious ointment — a public act of devotion by one publicly known as a sinner.

So the witnesses make the verse the entrance of the penitent woman. The lesson the expositors draw is the boldness of penitent faith and the grace that draws sinners to Christ. The woman, the witnesses note, was "a sinner" — known in the city for an immoral life, a woman of ill name; yet, hearing that Jesus was at Simon's table, she came, uninvited and unashamed of the company's scorn, bringing her alabaster box of precious ointment. The witnesses press the courage of this: she entered the house of a Pharisee, where she could expect only contempt, drawn by something stronger than shame — faith in the One who received sinners. The fathers and moderns debate her identity, the expositors note: Gill carefully distinguishes her from Mary Magdalene and from Mary of Bethany, holding this a separate event in Galilee, though some (like Lightfoot at v.47) identify her with Mary Magdalene. But her name matters less than her act: a notorious sinner, moved by love and penitence, comes to Christ with her costliest gift. The witnesses note the significance of the alabaster and the ointment — the best vessel and the precious perfume, the woman's treasure poured out in devotion. The verse thus introduces the great contrast of the scene: the sinful woman, drawn by love to Christ, against the self-righteous Pharisee who despised her; and it sets the stage for the lesson on forgiveness and love that her act would occasion.
38:
"And stood at his feet behind him weeping, and began to wash his feet with tears, and did wipe them with the hairs of her head, and kissed his feet, and anointed them with the ointment." The witnesses dwell on the woman's acts of love and humility. The grammatical-historical Gill and Barnes explain the posture: Christ "reclined at meal on a couch, with his feet put out behind," so that one could approach them. Gill notes she stood there "as being ashamed and afraid to look him in the face," weeping for her sins and "melted down with the love of Christ." Barnes describes the acts as those of love, humility, and penitence: washing his feet with tears "in the manner of a servant," and kissing them as "an emblem of love and affection."

The witnesses note the lavish, self-forgetful devotion: tears for washing, her hair for a towel, kisses, and precious ointment — every act an expression of love, sorrow for sin, and reverence for Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse a picture of penitent love in its fullest expression. The lesson the expositors draw is the overflowing devotion of a forgiven heart. The woman, the witnesses note, took the lowliest place — standing behind Christ at his feet, not daring to face him, ashamed of her sin yet drawn by love. And there she poured out a series of acts, each more lavish than the last: she wept, and her tears fell on his feet; having no towel, she wiped them with the hair of her head — her glory abased to serve him; she kissed his feet, again and again, the kiss of love and reverence; and she anointed them with her precious ointment. The witnesses press the depth of feeling: she was "melted down with the love of Christ," her tears flowing from a heart at once broken for sin and overflowing with love for the Saviour. Every act, the expositors note, bespeaks humility and devotion — the servant's task of washing feet, the abasement of using her hair, the affection of the kisses, the costliness of the ointment. This was no calculated display but the spontaneous outpouring of a soul that had found mercy. The witnesses note the contrast soon to be drawn with Simon, who offered Christ none of these courtesies. The verse thus sets before us the model of penitent love: the forgiven sinner, careless of shame and cost, lavishing upon Christ every token of a grateful and adoring heart.
39:
"Now when the Pharisee which had bidden him saw it, he spake within himself, saying, This man, if he were a prophet, would have known who and what manner of woman this is that toucheth him: for she is a sinner." The witnesses expose Simon's secret thought and his false reasoning. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains his logic: "if Jesus had been sent from God as a prophet, he supposed he would have known the character of the woman and would have rebuked her; because Jesus did not rebuke her, he inferred that he could not be acquainted with her character." Behind this lay the Pharisaic view: "the touch of a Gentile, or a person singularly wicked, they supposed to be polluting, and the Pharisees avoided it." But, Barnes notes, "Jesus did not refuse the society of the guilty; he came to save the lost."

The witnesses note the double error: Simon doubted Christ's prophetic discernment (when in fact Christ knew both the woman and Simon's own thoughts), and he assumed that a true prophet must spurn sinners rather than receive them.

So the witnesses make the verse the unveiling of the Pharisee's heart. The lesson the expositors draw is the false standard by which self-righteousness judges Christ. Simon, the witnesses note, "spake within himself" — his objection unspoken, a secret thought — reasoning that if Jesus were truly a prophet, he would have known this woman's sinful character and refused her touch. His error, the expositors press, was twofold. First, he was wrong about the facts: Christ knew perfectly well who the woman was — and knew, besides, Simon's own hidden thoughts, the very discernment Simon doubted. Second, and deeper, he misjudged the nature of a prophet and of God's grace: he assumed that holiness must mean separation from sinners, that the touch of the guilty defiles, and that a true man of God would drive such a woman away. But Christ, the witnesses note, came not to spurn sinners but to save them; he received the penitent, not because he failed to know her sin, but because he came to forgive it. The Pharisee's whole standard was self-righteous and loveless: he despised the woman and doubted Christ, blind to the mercy unfolding before him. The verse thus exposes the cold heart of the host — judging both Christ and the woman by a false rule — and sets the stage for Christ's answer, which would read Simon's secret thought, prove his prophetic discernment, and overturn his whole estimate of sin, love, and forgiveness.
40:
"And Jesus answering said unto him, Simon, I have somewhat to say unto thee. And he saith, Master, say on." The witnesses note that Christ answered Simon's unspoken thought, thereby proving the very prophetic discernment Simon doubted. The Reformed Gill presses the point: "Christ, being God omniscient, knew not only the character of this woman... but also the secret thoughts and reasonings of the Pharisee, and makes answer to them; which shows that he was a prophet, in the sense of this man — yea, more than a prophet." Calvin agrees: "exposing to public view his silent and concealed thought, he proves himself to possess something more excellent... he does not reply to his words, but refutes the sentiment which he kept hidden within his breast." Ellicott notes "Master, say on" recognizes Christ "as a teacher or Rabbi," and Robertson detects "a tone of Socratic irony" in Christ's approach.

The witnesses note the gentle courtesy of Christ's address — "Simon, I have somewhat to say unto thee" — and Simon's unsuspecting reply, "Master, say on," not realizing he was about to be answered in his own secret reasoning.

So the witnesses make the verse the turning-point, where Christ answers the thought Simon never spoke. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, by reading the Pharisee's heart, proves himself the very prophet — and more — that Simon doubted him to be. Simon had reasoned secretly that Jesus was no prophet, since he did not know the woman's character; but Christ, the witnesses note, now answers that secret reasoning itself — addressing not Simon's words (he had spoken none aloud) but the hidden sentiment of his heart. In doing so, the expositors press, Christ demonstrates a discernment far beyond what Simon required of a prophet: he knew not only the woman's sin but the Pharisee's unspoken thoughts, reading the heart as only God can. Gill's note, the witnesses observe, draws the conclusion Simon should have drawn: Jesus was indeed a prophet, "yea, more than a prophet" — the omniscient Lord who searches the heart. The gentle courtesy of Christ's approach, the expositors note — naming Simon, asking leave to speak — and the unsuspecting "Master, say on," lend the scene a quiet irony: the host who doubted his guest's discernment is about to be convicted by it, drawn unwittingly into the parable that will expose his own coldness. The verse thus opens Christ's answer, in which he will, by the parable of the two debtors, overturn Simon's whole judgment of the woman, of himself, and of the love that springs from forgiveness.
41:
"There was a certain creditor which had two debtors: the one owed five hundred pence, and the other fifty." The witnesses expound the parable of the two debtors. The grammatical-historical Barnes gives the values: "five hundred pence, about 69 dollars... fifty, about 7 dollars" (the denarius a day's wage). Adam Clarke draws the application: "by the creditor our Lord means God, and by the two debtors, Simon and the woman. Simon, who had the light of the law... might be considered the debtor who owed fifty pence; the woman, having no rule to regulate her actions, may be considered the debtor who owed five hundred." Yet, Clarke notes, "notwithstanding this great disparity, both were insolvent — Simon could no more pay his fifty to God than this poor heathen her five hundred."

The witnesses note the structure: two debtors, one owing ten times the other, both alike unable to pay — the parable framed to draw from Simon himself the principle that would convict him.

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of Christ's searching parable. The lesson the expositors draw is the common insolvency of all sinners before God, whatever the degree of their debt. The two debtors, the witnesses note, represent the two persons present: the sinful woman, whose debt was great (five hundred pence), and the self-righteous Pharisee, whose debt was smaller in his own and the world's esteem (fifty pence) — Simon having the restraints of the law and his religious profession, the woman having lived openly in sin. Yet, the expositors press with Clarke, the decisive point is that both were insolvent: neither could pay. Simon's smaller debt was as much beyond his power to discharge as the woman's larger debt beyond hers; for sin against an infinite God is a debt no finite creature can pay, and the respectable Pharisee stood as truly bankrupt before God as the notorious sinner. The witnesses note the wisdom of the parable's framing: by setting two debtors of unequal but alike unpayable debt, Christ prepares to draw from Simon's own mouth the verdict that will turn upon himself. The verse thus lays the foundation of the lesson — that all are debtors who cannot pay, the respectable and the disreputable alike — and prepares for the gracious turn: the creditor who freely forgives, and the love that springs from forgiveness.
42:
"And when they had nothing to pay, he frankly forgave them both. Tell me therefore, which of them will love him most?" The witnesses dwell on the free forgiveness and the question. The Reformed Gill expounds the insolvency and grace: "man has run out his whole stock... he has no money, no righteousness; and if he had, it would be nothing to pay with, since that itself is due to God; sin being committed against an infinite being, requires infinite satisfaction, which a finite creature can never give... but behold the wonderful grace of God, the creditor!" Barnes guards the analogy: "frankly forgave — freely, without any compensation. This is not designed to express the way in which God forgives sinners... it is crime which he forgives; he pardons as a moral governor," in connection with the atonement, whereas a creditor may remit a mere debt without an equivalent.

The question — "which will love him most?" — the witnesses note, is the hinge of the parable, designed to draw from Simon the principle of love proportioned to forgiveness.

So the witnesses make the verse the gracious heart of the parable. The lesson the expositors draw is the free forgiveness of the insolvent and the love it produces. Both debtors, the witnesses note, "had nothing to pay" — the picture of every sinner, who has no righteousness to offer God, for even his best is already owed, and his sin against the infinite God is a debt no finite creature can discharge. Into this hopeless insolvency comes grace: the creditor "frankly forgave them both" — freely, without compensation, cancelling the whole debt of his own bounty. The witnesses note Barnes's careful qualification: the parable illustrates the freeness of forgiveness, not its whole manner; for God forgives not a mere debt but crime, and pardons as a righteous moral governor, in connection with the atonement of Christ — so the analogy must not be pressed to deny the cross. The question Christ poses — "which of them will love him most?" — the expositors press, is the key: it lays down the principle that love answers to forgiveness, the greater pardon drawing the greater love. The witnesses note that this question, innocently answered by Simon, will turn upon him: for if love is the measure of forgiveness felt, then the woman's great love proved her great forgiveness, and Simon's coldness betrayed how little he thought himself to owe. The verse thus sets forth the gospel in miniature — free pardon to the insolvent — and the love that flows from it, preparing for Simon's self-condemning answer.
43:
"Simon answered and said, I suppose that he, to whom he forgave most. And he said unto him, Thou hast rightly judged." The witnesses note Simon's unwitting self-condemnation. The grammatical-historical Robertson catches the tone: "I suppose (an air of supercilious indifference); the most (the more); rightly (correctly) — Socrates was fond of 'quite rightly.'" Ellicott hears "a tone partly of indifference, partly of uneasiness as to what the drift of the parable might be." Barnes notes the result: "he saw not the point of our Lord's parable; by thus saying, he condemned himself, and prepared the way for our Lord's reproof." Wesley adds a caution: "neither of them will love him at all, before he has forgiven them; an insolvent debtor, till forgiven, does not love but fly his creditor."

Simon, the witnesses note, gives the correct but reluctant answer — the one forgiven most will love most — not perceiving that he was pronouncing judgment on himself.

So the witnesses make the verse Simon's self-condemning verdict. The lesson the expositors draw is how the self-righteous, asked to judge, unwittingly condemn themselves. Simon answers Christ's question correctly — "I suppose that he to whom he forgave most" will love most — and Christ approves: "Thou hast rightly judged." But the witnesses note the manner of his reply: a "supercilious indifference" (Robertson), an uneasy half-suspicion of where the parable was tending, the grudging "I suppose" of a man who senses a trap but cannot see it. In giving the right answer, the expositors press, Simon unknowingly pronounced his own sentence: he laid down the principle — love is proportioned to forgiveness felt — by which Christ would convict him, for the woman's great love proved her great forgiveness, while Simon's want of love betrayed how little he thought himself to need pardon. Wesley's caution, the witnesses observe, sharpens the order: love does not earn forgiveness but follows it; the unforgiven debtor flies his creditor, and only the pardoned soul loves. The verse thus turns the parable's point back upon the host: having judged rightly in the abstract, Simon has supplied the very rule by which his own coldness and the woman's devotion will be measured. Christ's "Thou hast rightly judged" is gentle, but it seals the verdict Simon has unwittingly passed on himself, and prepares for the pointed application that follows.
44:
"And he turned to the woman, and said unto Simon, Seest thou this woman? I entered into thine house, thou gavest me no water for my feet: but she hath washed my feet with tears, and wiped them with the hairs of her head." The witnesses note the pointed contrast Christ draws between the woman and the host. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains the custom: "among Eastern people it was customary, before eating, to wash the feet... to do this was one of the rites of hospitality" (Genesis 18:4; Judges 19:21), needful because "they wore sandals and reclined on couches." Clarke notes Simon's failure "whether from forgetfulness or contempt," and that "the custom of giving water to wash the guest's feet was very ancient." Ellicott draws the inference: "there had been no real respect or reverence in the Pharisee's invitation; it was hardly more than an act of ostentatious patronage."

The witnesses note the striking comparison: Simon omitted even the common courtesy of water for the feet, while the woman washed those feet with her tears and wiped them with her hair.

So the witnesses make the verse the first stroke of Christ's reproof of Simon by the woman's example. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between cold formality and warm love. Christ bids Simon "behold this woman" — the very one he despised — and sets her devotion against the host's neglect. The witnesses note the failure of common courtesy: it was the ordinary rite of hospitality to provide water for a guest's feet, dusty from sandalled travel; yet Simon, the expositors press, gave none — whether from forgetfulness or, more likely, from a contemptuous, patronizing spirit that invited Christ without honouring him. The woman, by contrast, did far more than the omitted courtesy: she washed Christ's feet not with water but with her tears, and wiped them not with a towel but with the hair of her head — the costliest and most humble service, where Simon had not offered even the cheapest. The witnesses note the depth of the rebuke: the despised sinner showed more true reverence for Christ than the religious Pharisee, her love supplying abundantly what his coldness had withheld. Ellicott's observation, the expositors observe, lays bare Simon's heart: his invitation was patronage, not honour. The verse thus begins the threefold contrast (feet, kiss, oil) by which Christ exposes the loveless host and vindicates the loving woman, drawing out the principle that love is the measure and fruit of forgiveness received.
45:
"Thou gavest me no kiss: but this woman since the time I came in hath not ceased to kiss my feet." The witnesses note the second contrast. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains "the kiss was a token of affection or a common mode of salutation," which Simon "had even neglected" (Genesis 33:4; Exodus 18:7). He draws the contrast: "how striking the difference between the conduct of Simon and this woman! He, with all the richness of a splendid preparation, had omitted the common marks of regard; she, in humility, had bowed at his feet and had not ceased to kiss them." Clarke notes a textual point — better "since the time she came in" — for the woman entered after Christ.

The witnesses note the intensity of the woman's devotion: not a single kiss of greeting (which Simon omitted), but a continual kissing of Christ's feet, the lowliest place, since she entered.

So the witnesses make the verse the second stroke of the contrast. The lesson the expositors draw is again the difference between the host's cold neglect and the woman's fervent love. The kiss, the witnesses note, was the common token of welcome and affection — a courtesy Simon owed his guest and failed to give; he received Christ without even the ordinary greeting of regard. The woman, by contrast, did far more: she "hath not ceased to kiss" Christ's feet — not the cheek, in the manner of an equal's greeting, but the feet, in the posture of deepest humility; and not once, but continually, from the moment she entered. The witnesses press Barnes's striking observation: Simon, with all the lavishness of a splendid banquet, omitted the simplest mark of affection, while the woman, in lowly love, never ceased her humble homage. The expositors note the abiding lesson: the most costly outward entertainment may lack the love that even the poorest devotion supplies; Simon's rich table was empty of the regard that the woman's kisses poured out. And, the witnesses observe, no motive of self-interest could explain her act — only love could move a broken sinner to such continual, humble devotion. The verse thus advances the contrast: where Simon gave not even a kiss of greeting, the woman covered Christ's feet with kisses — her love the measure of the forgiveness she had received, and the rebuke of the host's loveless formality.
46:
"My head with oil thou didst not anoint: but this woman hath anointed my feet with ointment." The witnesses note the third and climactic contrast. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains the custom: "the custom of pouring oil upon the head was universal among the Jews... sweet oil or oil of olives" (Ruth 3:3; 2 Samuel 12:20; Psalm 23:5), while "this ointment was a mixture of aromatics, far more costly and precious." He draws the contrast: "he did not give even the common oil for his head; she had applied to his feet a far more precious unguent — he showed comparatively little love, she showed much." Clarke confirms anointing the head with oil "was as common among the Jews as washing the face with water is among us."

The witnesses note the mounting disparity: Simon withheld even the cheap oil for the head; the woman lavished costly ointment on the feet — the lowest part, with the most precious gift.

So the witnesses make the verse the climax of the threefold contrast. The lesson the expositors draw is the full measure of the difference between the loveless host and the loving penitent. The witnesses note the rising scale of the comparison: Simon omitted water for the feet, the kiss of greeting, and now even the common oil for the head — the cheap olive oil that hospitality and courtesy ordinarily provided (Psalm 23:5). The woman, by contrast, did the opposite at every point: not water but tears, not a kiss of the cheek but ceaseless kisses of the feet, and not common oil for the head but precious, costly ointment for the feet. The expositors press the deliberate inversion: Simon withheld the least at the highest place (oil for the head); the woman lavished the most at the lowest (ointment for the feet). The disparity, the witnesses note, lays bare the hearts: Simon "showed comparatively little love," doing not even the ordinary courtesies; the woman "showed much," sparing nothing, abasing herself to the feet and pouring out her treasure. And love, the expositors observe, is the index of forgiveness felt — so this great love bespoke a great forgiveness received, while Simon's want of love betrayed his sense that he owed little. The verse thus completes the contrast that Christ has drawn, and prepares for the application in which he will name the principle directly: her many sins are forgiven, for she loved much; but to whom little is forgiven, the same loveth little.
47:
"Wherefore I say unto thee, Her sins, which are many, are forgiven; for she loved much: but to whom little is forgiven, the same loveth little." The witnesses confront the crucial question: is love the cause or the evidence of forgiveness? The Reformed Gill and Lightfoot insist love is the fruit, not the ground: Gill notes Christ does not say her sins are forgiven because she loved, but speaks "to remove Simon's mistake... she was a pardoned sinner," her love the proof. Lightfoot identifies the order: "this was not the first time she betook herself to our Saviour, nor the first of her receiving remission... having been obliged by so great a mercy, she now throws herself in gratitude at the feet of Christ" — the love following the pardon already received. The whole parable, the witnesses note, teaches that the forgiven love, not that the loving are thereby forgiven; "for" introduces the evidence ("you may know she is forgiven, for she loves"), not the cause.

Christ's closing clause confirms it: "to whom little is forgiven, the same loveth little" — Simon's want of love betraying not a smaller debt but a smaller sense of pardon.

So the witnesses make the verse the doctrinal heart of the passage. The lesson the expositors draw is that love is the fruit and evidence of forgiveness, not its purchasing cause. The crux, the witnesses note, lies in the word "for": "her sins are forgiven, for she loved much." Read carelessly, this might seem to make her love the ground of her pardon — as though God forgave her because she loved. But the witnesses press, with Gill and Lightfoot and the whole tenor of the parable, that this cannot be the meaning. The parable itself teaches the opposite order: the debtors were forgiven first, freely, when they had nothing to pay, and love followed the forgiveness — the more forgiven, the more love. So the woman's great love was not the cause but the proof of a great forgiveness already received; she had come to Christ before, found mercy, and now poured out her love in grateful response. The "for," the expositors explain, is evidential, not causal: "you may know she is forgiven, for she loves much" — love being the sign by which the pardoned heart is read. And the contrast confirms it: "to whom little is forgiven, the same loveth little" — Simon's coldness betrayed, not that he owed little, but that he felt little need of pardon and so loved little. The witnesses note that to reverse the order — making love earn forgiveness — would overthrow the gospel of free grace the parable was framed to teach. The verse thus declares the woman pardoned, her love the evidence; and warns, in Simon, that the loveless heart is the unforgiven heart, because it has never felt its debt.
48:
"And he said unto her, Thy sins are forgiven." The witnesses note Christ's direct word of pardon to the woman and the deity it implies. The grammatical-historical Barnes presses the point: "what a gracious assurance to the weeping, loving, penitent! How that voice, spoken to the troubled sinner, stills his anguish... And how manifest is it that he that could say thus must be God! No man has a right to forgive sin... Here Jesus gave indubitable proof that he was God as well as man." Ellicott notes the perfect tense — "thy sins have been forgiven" — and that the words "did not for the first time convey forgiveness; that had been sealed before by an unspoken absolution," but now openly assured her.

Christ, the witnesses note, having vindicated the woman to Simon, now turns to her directly with the open word of pardon — confirming aloud the forgiveness she had already received, for her comfort and assurance.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's gracious assurance of pardon. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the comfort of the spoken word: Christ, having defended the woman against Simon's scorn, now addresses her directly — "Thy sins are forgiven" — sealing with his own voice the pardon she had come seeking and already received. The witnesses note the tenderness: to the weeping, loving penitent, this word stills all anguish, allays her fears, and gives the settled peace of one whose sins are put away. Ellicott's note on the tense, the expositors observe, guards the order taught at verse 47: the forgiveness was not first conveyed now, but had been granted before (her love being its evidence); yet Christ speaks it openly for her assurance, that she might go in peace knowing her pardon sure. Second, and weightier, the deity implied: Barnes presses that to forgive sins is the prerogative of God alone — no man may forgive sin against God, nor speak peace to the conscience; so that Christ, in pronouncing this pardon, gave "indubitable proof that he was God as well as man," Lord of the conscience and able to read and to heal the heart. The verse thus joins comfort and confession: comfort to the penitent, assured of pardon; and a quiet claim of deity, which the very company would catch in the next verse, asking "Who is this that even forgiveth sins?"
49:
"And they that sat at meat with him began to say within themselves, Who is this that forgiveth sins also?" The witnesses note the guests' astonishment and the implicit charge. The grammatical-historical Ellicott renders it "Who is this that even forgiveth sins?" and notes "the thought that underlay the question... was the same as that which had found utterance" at Capernaum (Luke 5:21; Mark 2:6). Robertson recalls that "once before the Pharisees considered Jesus guilty of blasphemy in claiming the power to forgive sins (Luke 5:21); Jesus read their inmost thoughts as he always does." Barnes calls it "a very pertinent question: who could he be but God? Man could not do it, and there is no wonder that they were amazed."

The guests, the witnesses note, reasoned within themselves — as Simon had earlier — at the audacity of one who claimed to forgive sins, a power they rightly knew belonged to God alone.

So the witnesses make the verse the reaction of the table to Christ's word of pardon. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ's forgiveness raised, even in his critics, the question of his deity. The guests "began to say within themselves" — secretly, as Simon had reasoned — "Who is this that even forgiveth sins?" The witnesses note the rightness of their premise, even in their unbelief: they knew that to forgive sins committed against God is God's prerogative alone, and so the claim was either blasphemy or proof of divinity. Barnes presses the inescapable conclusion: "who could he be but God? Man could not do it" — so that their very astonishment, had they followed it honestly, pointed to the truth. The witnesses note, with Robertson, that this was the same charge of implicit blasphemy raised at Capernaum (Luke 5:21), where the Pharisees had murmured at the like claim; and there, as here, Christ read their secret thoughts, the very omniscience that confirmed his right to forgive. The expositors observe the tragedy: the guests asked the right question — "Who is this?" — but did not follow it to the right answer, content to murmur their offence rather than own their Lord. The verse thus shows Christ's pardon provoking the question of his identity; and the answer they would not draw — that he is God, with authority on earth to forgive sins — the reader is left to confess. Christ, undeterred by their murmuring, turns once more to the woman with the final word of peace.
50:
"And he said to the woman, Thy faith hath saved thee; go in peace." The witnesses note Christ's final word, ascribing salvation to faith. The grammatical-historical Barnes cross-references the like word to others (Mark 5:34; Matthew 9:22; Luke 8:48; 18:42), and Ephesians 2:8 — saved through faith. Clarke explains: "thy faith hath been the instrument of receiving the salvation promised to those who repent." On "go in peace," he notes it is "a valediction or farewell," as in the Hebrew idiom (1 Samuel 1:17; 20:42), yet here joined to the real peace of conscience that pardon brings.

The witnesses note the order confirmed: not "thy love hath saved thee," but "thy faith" — faith, not the love that flowed from it, being the instrument of salvation; and "go in peace," the dismissal of one whose sins are pardoned and conscience set at rest.

So the witnesses make the verse the close of the scene and the seal of the woman's salvation. The lesson the expositors draw is that faith, not love nor works, is the instrument by which the sinner receives forgiveness. The witnesses note the precision of Christ's word: he says "thy faith hath saved thee" — not "thy love," though her love had been so conspicuous. This confirms the order taught throughout: her love was the fruit and evidence of her pardon (v.47), but it was faith that laid hold of the salvation Christ gave. The expositors press the doctrinal point, against any reading that would make her love the cause of her forgiveness: salvation is received through faith (Ephesians 2:8), and the love, however great, was its consequence, not its ground. The witnesses note the tender dismissal, "go in peace" — at once a customary farewell and, here, the bestowal of real peace: the peace of a forgiven conscience, the inseparable fruit of pardon. The woman who had come weeping, bowed in shame at Christ's feet, now departs in peace, her sins forgiven, her faith honoured, her love accepted. The verse thus crowns the whole account: against the murmuring guests and the cold host, Christ speaks salvation and peace to the penitent believer, sealing the gospel the chapter has unfolded — that the sinner who comes to Christ in faith is forgiven freely, and goes in peace, loving much because she has been forgiven much.
